# Evidence registry

This registry records the sources, scope, and limitations for empirical claims used by GARDEN documentation and plugin skills.

## CLAIM-N001 — Instruction-file compliance and runtime enforcement

Verdict: practitioner-report

### Statement

Rules kept only in an instruction file were followed less reliably than the same rules enforced by a runtime hook or deterministic gate in one practitioner's sessions.

### Source

Merlin Mann, ["The 30% Problem"](https://gist.github.com/merlinmann/34708a72be7ed2e3aad013a2bbeb7f83), published 2026-04-07.

### Population

166 Claude Code sessions from one author, assessed across five self-defined behavioral dimensions.

### Measured result

The report gives 25-40% compliance for rules stated only in an instruction file and about 95% when the same rule is enforced by a runtime hook or deterministic gate.

### Limitations

This is an informal, single-author practitioner report, not peer-reviewed research. It has no independent replication. It does not verify separate task-success figures for curated or autogenerated context files.

### Used by

- `docs/explanation/the-garden-model.md`

## CLAIM-N002 — Chroma context rot

Verdict: trend-only

### Statement

Model performance can degrade as input context grows, including on simple retrieval tasks.

### Source

Chroma Research, ["Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance"](https://research.trychroma.com/context-rot), Kelly Hong, Anton Troynikov, and Jeff Huber, July 2025. The accompanying code is at [chroma-core/context-rot](https://github.com/chroma-core/context-rot).

### Population

Eighteen frontier models, including GPT-4.1, Claude Opus 4, and Gemini 2.5, evaluated with needle-question similarity, distractor interference, haystack structure, LongMemEval, and repeated-word tasks.

### Measured result

All evaluated models degraded with greater input length, including on trivial tasks. The report observed lost-in-the-middle accuracy drops above 30% and better performance on shuffled than coherent haystacks.

### Limitations

The phenomenon is supported by the report, but its experiments do not establish that degradation is primarily a state-tracking failure rather than a reasoning failure. That causal framing is GARDEN's interpretation.

### Used by

- `docs/explanation/why-agent-first-principles.md`
- `docs/reference/glossary.md`
- `docs/reference/principles.md`

## CLAIM-N003 — CodeX-Verify multi-agent verification

Verdict: trend-only

### Statement

One single-author preprint reports higher defect-detection performance for its own four-agent CodeX-Verify system than for a single agent.

### Source

Shreshth Rajan, ["Multi-Agent Code Verification via Information Theory"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.16708), arXiv:2511.16708, November 2025.

### Population

Ninety-nine code samples with verified labels, evaluated with the author's four-agent CodeX-Verify system and all 15 agent combinations.

### Measured result

The preprint reports 32.8% for one agent and 72.4% for the full system, a reported 39.7 percentage-point improvement. It reports diminishing returns of 14.9, 13.5, and 11.2 percentage points for added agents. Its system-level false-positive rate is 50%, compared with 8.6% for test-based verification.

### Limitations

This is a single-author preprint on the author's own system and sample set, not independent evidence that multi-agent review generally works. It does not confirm the frequently repeated false-negative figure.

### Used by

- `docs/explanation/why-agent-first-principles.md`
- `docs/how-to/review-code-as-agent.md`
- `docs/how-to/set-up-verification-gates.md`
- `docs/reference/principles.md`
- `plugins/garden/skills/review/SKILL.md`

## CLAIM-N004 — GitClear AI Copilot Code Quality

Verdict: confirmed

### Statement

GitClear observed changes in code-churn and clone-detection metrics over 2020-2024; the report does not establish that any architecture practice caused those changes.

### Source

GitClear, ["AI Copilot Code Quality"](https://www.gitclear.com/ai_assistant_code_quality_2025_research), February 2025.

### Population

211 million changed lines from mixed commercial codebases, including NextGen Health and Bank of Georgia, and open-source codebases, including React, Chromium, and VS Code. The data period is January 2020 through December 2024.

### Measured result

Copy-pasted code increased from 8.3% in 2020 to 12.3% in 2024. Refactored or moved code declined from 24.1% to 9.5%, and duplicated blocks of five or more lines grew roughly eightfold during 2020-2024.

### Limitations

These are observational git-diff churn and clone-detection metrics. They show correlation in the measured corpus, not a causal effect of AI assistants or a proof that any specific architecture practice prevents duplication.

### Used by

- `docs/explanation/relation-to-classic-principles.md`
- `docs/explanation/the-garden-model.md`
- `docs/explanation/why-agent-first-principles.md`
- `docs/reference/principles.md`
